question:
Given a list of the positive integers $1,2,3,4,\dots$, take the first three numbers
$1,2,3$ and their sum $6$ and cross all four numbers off the list. Repeat with the three 
smallest remaining numbers $4,5,7$ and their sum $16$. Continue in this way, crossing off the three smallest remaining numbers and their sum, and consider the sequence of sums produced: $6, 16, 27, 36, \dots$. Prove or disprove that there is some number in the sequence whose base 10 representation ends with $2015$.

\,
solution:
We will prove that 42015 is such a number in the sequence.
Label the sequence of sums $s_0, s_1, \dots$, and let $a_n, b_n, c_n$ be the summands of $s_n$ in ascending order. We prove the following two statements for each nonnegative integer $n$: \begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)$_n$]
The sequence
\[
a_{3n}, b_{3n}, c_{3n}, a_{3n+1}, b_{3n+1}, c_{3n+1},
a_{3n+2}, b_{3n+2}, c_{3n+2}
\]
is obtained from the sequence $10n+1, \dots, 10n+10$ by removing one of $10n+5, 10n+6, 10n+7$.
\item[(b)$_n$]
We have
\begin{align*}
s_{3n} &= 30n+6,\\
s_{3n+1} &\in \{30n+15, 30n+16, 30n+17\}, \\
s_{3n+2} &= 30n+27.
\end{align*}
\end{enumerate}
These statements follow by induction from the following simple observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item
by computing the table of values
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
n & a_n & b_n & c_n & s_n \\
\hline
 0 &  1 &  2 &  3 &  6 \\
 1 &  4 &  5 &  7 & 16 \\
 2 &  8 &  9 & 10 & 27
\end{array}
\]
we see that (a)$_0$ holds;
\item
(a)$_n$ implies (b)$_n$;
\item
(a)$_n$ and (b)$_1$, \dots, (b)$_n$ together imply (a)$_{n+1}$.
\end{itemize}
To produce a value of $n$ for which $s_n \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$, we take $n = 3m+1$ for some nonnegative integer $m$ for which $s_{3m+1} = 30m+15$. We must also have $30m \equiv 2000 \pmod{10000}$, or equivalently $m \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$.
By taking $m = 1400$, we ensure that $m \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, so $s_m = 10m + 7$; this ensures that $s_{n}$ does indeed equal $30m+15 = 42015$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
With a bit more work, we can give a complete description of $s_n$, and in particular find the first term in the sequence whose decimal expansion ends in 2015.
Define the function on nonnegative integers
\[
f(n) = s_{3n+1} - (30n + 16)
\]
which takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1\}$; we then have
\[
f(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & n \equiv 0 \pmod{3} \\
-f((n-1)/3) & n \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \\
-1 & n \equiv 2 \pmod{3}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, if we write $n$ in base 3, then $f(n) = 0$ unless the expansion ends with 2 followed by a string of 1s of length $k \geq 0$, in which case $f(n) = (-1)^{k+1}$.

In this notation, we have $s_n \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$ if and only if $n = 3m+1$
for some nonnegative integer $m$ for which
$m \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$ and $f(m) = -1$.
Since $400 = 112211_{(3)}$, the first such term in the sequence is in fact $s_{1201} = 12015$.

descriptive_long.question:
Given a list of the positive integers $1,2,3,4,\dots$, take the first three numbers
$1,2,3$ and their sum $6$ and cross all four numbers off the list. Repeat with the three 
smallest remaining numbers $4,5,7$ and their sum $16$. Continue in this way, crossing off the three smallest remaining numbers and their sum, and consider the sequence of sums produced: $6, 16, 27, 36, \dots$. Prove or disprove that there is some number in the sequence whose base 10 representation ends with $2015$.
descriptive_long.solution:
We will prove that 42015 is such a number in the sequence.
Label the sequence of sums $sumseq_0, sumseq_1, \dots$, and let $firstsummand_{indexvar}, secondsummand_{indexvar}, thirdsummand_{indexvar}$ be the summands of $sumseq_{indexvar}$ in ascending order. We prove the following two statements for each nonnegative integer $indexvar$:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)$_{indexvar}$]
The sequence
\[
firstsummand_{3indexvar}, secondsummand_{3indexvar}, thirdsummand_{3indexvar}, firstsummand_{3indexvar+1}, secondsummand_{3indexvar+1}, thirdsummand_{3indexvar+1},
firstsummand_{3indexvar+2}, secondsummand_{3indexvar+2}, thirdsummand_{3indexvar+2}
\]
is obtained from the sequence $10indexvar+1, \dots, 10indexvar+10$ by removing one of $10indexvar+5, 10indexvar+6, 10indexvar+7$.
\item[(b)$_{indexvar}$]
We have
\begin{align*}
sumseq_{3indexvar} &= 30indexvar+6,\\
sumseq_{3indexvar+1} &\in \{30indexvar+15, 30indexvar+16, 30indexvar+17\}, \\
sumseq_{3indexvar+2} &= 30indexvar+27.
\end{align*}
\end{enumerate}
These statements follow by induction from the following simple observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item by computing the table of values
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
indexvar & firstsummand_{indexvar} & secondsummand_{indexvar} & thirdsummand_{indexvar} & sumseq_{indexvar} \\
\hline
 0 &  1 &  2 &  3 &  6 \\
 1 &  4 &  5 &  7 & 16 \\
 2 &  8 &  9 & 10 & 27
\end{array}
\]
we see that (a)$_0$ holds;
\item (a)$_{indexvar}$ implies (b)$_{indexvar}$;
\item (a)$_{indexvar}$ and (b)$_1$, \dots, (b)$_{indexvar}$ together imply (a)$_{indexvar+1}$.
\end{itemize}
To produce a value of $indexvar$ for which $sumseq_{indexvar} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$, we take $indexvar = 3auxindex+1$ for some nonnegative integer $auxindex$ for which $sumseq_{3auxindex+1} = 30auxindex+15$. We must also have $30auxindex \equiv 2000 \pmod{10000}$, or equivalently $auxindex \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$.
By taking $auxindex = 1400$, we ensure that $auxindex \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, so $sumseq_{auxindex} = 10auxindex + 7$; this ensures that $sumseq_{indexvar}$ does indeed equal $30auxindex+15 = 42015$, as desired.

\noindent\textbf{Remark:} With a bit more work, we can give a complete description of $sumseq_{indexvar}$, and in particular find the first term in the sequence whose decimal expansion ends in 2015.
Define the function on nonnegative integers
\[
modfunc(indexvar) = sumseq_{3indexvar+1} - (30indexvar + 16)
\]
which takes values in $\{-1,0,1\}$; we then have
\[
modfunc(indexvar)=\begin{cases}0 & indexvar \equiv 0 \pmod{3}\\-\,modfunc((indexvar-1)/3) & indexvar \equiv 1 \pmod{3}\\-1 & indexvar \equiv 2 \pmod{3}.\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, if we write $indexvar$ in base 3, then $modfunc(indexvar)=0$ unless the expansion ends with 2 followed by a string of 1s of length $onescount \ge 0$, in which case $modfunc(indexvar)=(-1)^{onescount+1}$.

In this notation, we have $sumseq_{indexvar} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$ iff $indexvar = 3auxindex+1$ for some nonnegative integer $auxindex$ with $auxindex \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$ and $modfunc(auxindex)=-1$. Since $400 = 112211_{(3)}$, the first such term in the sequence is in fact $sumseq_{1201}=12015$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Given a list of the positive integers $1,2,3,4,\dots$, take the first three numbers
$1,2,3$ and their sum $6$ and cross all four numbers off the list. Repeat with the three 
smallest remaining numbers $4,5,7$ and their sum $16$. Continue in this way, crossing off the three smallest remaining numbers and their sum, and consider the sequence of sums produced: $6, 16, 27, 36, \dots$. Prove or disprove that there is some number in the sequence whose base 10 representation ends with $2015$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We will prove that 42015 is such a number in the sequence.
Label the sequence of sums $blackboard_0, blackboard_1, \dots$, and let $pineapple_{rainstorm}, jellyfish_{rainstorm}, arrowhead_{rainstorm}$ be the summands of $blackboard_{rainstorm}$ in ascending order. We prove the following two statements for each nonnegative integer $rainstorm$: 
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)$_{rainstorm}$]
The sequence
\[
pineapple_{3rainstorm}, jellyfish_{3rainstorm}, arrowhead_{3rainstorm}, \pineapple_{3rainstorm+1}, jellyfish_{3rainstorm+1}, arrowhead_{3rainstorm+1},
\pineapple_{3rainstorm+2}, jellyfish_{3rainstorm+2}, arrowhead_{3rainstorm+2}
\]
is obtained from the sequence $10rainstorm+1, \dots, 10rainstorm+10$ by removing one of $10rainstorm+5, 10rainstorm+6, 10rainstorm+7$.
\item[(b)$_{rainstorm}$]
We have
\begin{align*}
blackboard_{3rainstorm} &= 30rainstorm+6,\\
blackboard_{3rainstorm+1} &\in \{30rainstorm+15, 30rainstorm+16, 30rainstorm+17\}, \\
blackboard_{3rainstorm+2} &= 30rainstorm+27.
\end{align*}
\end{enumerate}
These statements follow by induction from the following simple observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item
by computing the table of values
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
rainstorm & pineapple_{rainstorm} & jellyfish_{rainstorm} & arrowhead_{rainstorm} & blackboard_{rainstorm} \\
\hline
 0 &  1 &  2 &  3 &  6 \\
 1 &  4 &  5 &  7 & 16 \\
 2 &  8 &  9 & 10 & 27
\end{array}
\]
we see that (a)$_0$ holds;
\item
(a)$_{rainstorm}$ implies (b)$_{rainstorm}$;
\item
(a)$_{rainstorm}$ and (b)$_1$, \dots, (b)$_{rainstorm}$ together imply (a)$_{rainstorm+1}$.
\end{itemize}
To produce a value of $rainstorm$ for which $blackboard_{rainstorm} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$, we take $rainstorm = 3lighthouse+1$ for some nonnegative integer $lighthouse$ for which $blackboard_{3lighthouse+1} = 30lighthouse+15$. We must also have $30lighthouse \equiv 2000 \pmod{10000}$, or equivalently $lighthouse \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$.
By taking $lighthouse = 1400$, we ensure that $lighthouse \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, so $blackboard_{lighthouse} = 10lighthouse + 7$; this ensures that $blackboard_{rainstorm}$ does indeed equal $30lighthouse+15 = 42015$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
With a bit more work, we can give a complete description of $blackboard_{rainstorm}$, and in particular find the first term in the sequence whose decimal expansion ends in 2015.
Define the function on nonnegative integers
\[
tangerine(rainstorm) = blackboard_{3rainstorm+1} - (30rainstorm + 16)
\]
which takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1\}$; we then have
\[
tangerine(rainstorm) = \begin{cases} 0 & rainstorm \equiv 0 \pmod{3} \\
-\,tangerine((rainstorm-1)/3) & rainstorm \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \\
-1 & rainstorm \equiv 2 \pmod{3}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, if we write $rainstorm$ in base 3, then $tangerine(rainstorm) = 0$ unless the expansion ends with 2 followed by a string of 1s of length $blueberry \geq 0$, in which case $tangerine(rainstorm) = (-1)^{blueberry+1}$.

In this notation, we have $blackboard_{rainstorm} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$ if and only if $rainstorm = 3lighthouse+1$
for some nonnegative integer $lighthouse$ for which
$lighthouse \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$ and $tangerine(lighthouse) = -1$.
Since $400 = 112211_{(3)}$, the first such term in the sequence is in fact $blackboard_{1201} = 12015$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Given a list of the positive integers $1,2,3,4,\dots$, take the first three numbers
$1,2,3$ and their sum $6$ and cross all four numbers off the list. Repeat with the three 
smallest remaining numbers $4,5,7$ and their sum $16$. Continue in this way, crossing off the three smallest remaining numbers and their sum, and consider the sequence of sums produced: $6, 16, 27, 36, \dots$. Prove or disprove that there is some number in the sequence whose base 10 representation ends with $2015$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We will prove that 42015 is such a number in the sequence.
Label the sequence of sums $differencevalue_0, differencevalue_1, \dots$, and let $largestpart_{fractional}, outerpart_{fractional}, smallestpart_{fractional}$ be the summands of $differencevalue_{fractional}$ in ascending order. We prove the following two statements for each nonnegative integer $fractional$: \begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)$_{fractional}$]
The sequence
\[
largestpart_{3\fractional}, outerpart_{3\fractional}, smallestpart_{3\fractional}, largestpart_{3\fractional+1}, outerpart_{3\fractional+1}, smallestpart_{3\fractional+1},
largestpart_{3\fractional+2}, outerpart_{3\fractional+2}, smallestpart_{3\fractional+2}
\]
is obtained from the sequence $10\fractional+1, \dots, 10\fractional+10$ by removing one of $10\fractional+5, 10\fractional+6, 10\fractional+7$.
\item[(b)$_{fractional}$]
We have
\begin{align*}
differencevalue_{3\fractional} &= 30\fractional+6,\\
differencevalue_{3\fractional+1} &\in \{30\fractional+15, 30\fractional+16, 30\fractional+17\}, \\
differencevalue_{3\fractional+2} &= 30\fractional+27.
\end{align*}
\end{enumerate}
These statements follow by induction from the following simple observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item
by computing the table of values
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
fractional & largestpart_{fractional} & outerpart_{fractional} & smallestpart_{fractional} & differencevalue_{fractional} \\
\hline
 0 &  1 &  2 &  3 &  6 \\
 1 &  4 &  5 &  7 & 16 \\
 2 &  8 &  9 & 10 & 27
\end{array}
\]
we see that (a)$_0$ holds;
\item
(a)$_{fractional}$ implies (b)$_{fractional}$;
\item
(a)$_{fractional}$ and (b)$_1$, \dots, (b)$_{fractional}$ together imply (a)$_{fractional+1}$.
\end{itemize}
To produce a value of $fractional$ for which $differencevalue_{fractional} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$, we take $fractional = 3\irrational+1$ for some nonnegative integer $\irrational$ for which $differencevalue_{3\irrational+1} = 30\irrational+15$. We must also have $30\irrational \equiv 2000 \pmod{10000}$, or equivalently $\irrational \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$.
By taking $\irrational = 1400$, we ensure that $\irrational \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, so $differencevalue_{\irrational} = 10\irrational + 7$; this ensures that $differencevalue_{fractional}$ does indeed equal $30\irrational+15 = 42015$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
With a bit more work, we can give a complete description of $differencevalue_{fractional}$, and in particular find the first term in the sequence whose decimal expansion ends in 2015.
Define the function on nonnegative integers
\[
constantvalue(fractional) = differencevalue_{3fractional+1} - (30fractional + 16)
\]
which takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1\}$; we then have
\[
constantvalue(fractional) = \begin{cases} 0 & fractional \equiv 0 \pmod{3} \\ -constantvalue((fractional-1)/3) & fractional \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \\ -1 & fractional \equiv 2 \pmod{3}. \end{cases}
\]
Consequently, if we write $fractional$ in base 3, then $constantvalue(fractional) = 0$ unless the expansion ends with 2 followed by a string of 1s of length $shortness \geq 0$, in which case $constantvalue(fractional) = (-1)^{shortness+1}$.

In this notation, we have $differencevalue_{fractional} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$ if and only if $fractional = 3\irrational+1$
for some nonnegative integer $\irrational$ for which
$\irrational \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$ and $constantvalue(\irrational) = -1$.
Since $400 = 112211_{(3)}$, the first such term in the sequence is in fact $differencevalue_{1201} = 12015$.

garbled_string.question: Given a list of the positive integers $1,2,3,4,\dots$, take the first three numbers $1,2,3$ and their sum $6$ and cross all four numbers off the list. Repeat with the three smallest remaining numbers $4,5,7$ and their sum $16$. Continue in this way, crossing off the three smallest remaining numbers and their sum, and consider the sequence of sums produced: $6, 16, 27, 36, \dots$. Prove or disprove that there is some number in the sequence whose base 10 representation ends with $2015$.
garbled_string.solution:
We will prove that 42015 is such a number in the sequence.
Label the sequence of sums $qzxwvtnp_0, qzxwvtnp_1, \dots$, and let $plwkojne_{pvztslry}, afygmntr_{pvztslry}, xzhqivsa_{pvztslry}$ be the summands of $qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry}$ in ascending order. We prove the following two statements for each nonnegative integer $pvztslry$:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)$_{pvztslry}$]
The sequence
\[
plwkojne_{3pvztslry}, afygmntr_{3pvztslry}, xzhqivsa_{3pvztslry}, plwkojne_{3pvztslry+1}, afygmntr_{3pvztslry+1}, xzhqivsa_{3pvztslry+1},
plwkojne_{3pvztslry+2}, afygmntr_{3pvztslry+2}, xzhqivsa_{3pvztslry+2}
\]
is obtained from the sequence $10pvztslry+1, \dots, 10pvztslry+10$ by removing one of $10pvztslry+5, 10pvztslry+6, 10pvztslry+7$.
\item[(b)$_{pvztslry}$]
We have
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp_{3pvztslry} &= 30pvztslry+6,\\
qzxwvtnp_{3pvztslry+1} &\in \{30pvztslry+15, 30pvztslry+16, 30pvztslry+17\}, \\
qzxwvtnp_{3pvztslry+2} &= 30pvztslry+27.
\end{align*}
\end{enumerate}
These statements follow by induction from the following simple observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item
by computing the table of values
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
pvztslry & plwkojne_{pvztslry} & afygmntr_{pvztslry} & xzhqivsa_{pvztslry} & qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry} \\\hline
 0 &  1 &  2 &  3 &  6 \\
 1 &  4 &  5 &  7 & 16 \\
 2 &  8 &  9 & 10 & 27
\end{array}
\]
we see that (a)$_0$ holds;
\item
(a)$_{pvztslry}$ implies (b)$_{pvztslry}$;
\item
(a)$_{pvztslry}$ and (b)$_1$, \dots, (b)$_{pvztslry}$ together imply (a)$_{pvztslry+1}$.
\end{itemize}
To produce a value of $pvztslry$ for which $qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$, we take $pvztslry = 3zcvbmads+1$ for some nonnegative integer $zcvbmads$ for which $qzxwvtnp_{3zcvbmads+1} = 30zcvbmads+15$. We must also have $30zcvbmads \equiv 2000 \pmod{10000}$, or equivalently $zcvbmads \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$.
By taking $zcvbmads = 1400$, we ensure that $zcvbmads \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, so $qzxwvtnp_{zcvbmads} = 10zcvbmads + 7$; this ensures that $qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry}$ does indeed equal $30zcvbmads+15 = 42015$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
With a bit more work, we can give a complete description of $qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry}$, and in particular find the first term in the sequence whose decimal expansion ends in 2015.
Define the function on nonnegative integers
\[
jdksalqp(pvztslry) = qzxwvtnp_{3pvztslry+1} - (30pvztslry + 16)
\]
which takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1\}$; we then have
\[
jdksalqp(pvztslry) = \begin{cases} 0 & pvztslry \equiv 0 \pmod{3} \\
-jdksalqp((pvztslry-1)/3) & pvztslry \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \\
-1 & pvztslry \equiv 2 \pmod{3}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, if we write $pvztslry$ in base 3, then $jdksalqp(pvztslry) = 0$ unless the expansion ends with 2 followed by a string of 1s of length $hgflpwei \geq 0$, in which case $jdksalqp(pvztslry) = (-1)^{hgflpwei+1}$.

In this notation, we have $qzxwvtnp_{pvztslry} \equiv 2015 \pmod{10000}$ if and only if $pvztslry = 3zcvbmads+1$
for some nonnegative integer $zcvbmads$ for which
$zcvbmads \equiv 400 \pmod{1000}$ and $jdksalqp(zcvbmads) = -1$.
Since $400 = 112211_{(3)}$, the first such term in the sequence is in fact $qzxwvtnp_{1201} = 12015$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(s_n)_{n\ge 0}$ be defined as follows.  Start with the ordered list of positive integers
$$1,2,3,4,\dots .$$
Repeatedly perform the two actions
 * cross out the three smallest numbers that have not yet been crossed out, and
 * cross out their sum,
and record that sum.  More precisely, at step $n$ we erase three still-present
integers $a_n\le b_n\le c_n$ and their sum
$$s_n=a_n+b_n+c_n.$$
The first few erasures are
$$(1,2,3,6),\;(4,5,7,16),\;(8,9,10,27),\;(11,12,13,36),\dots.$$
Thus $s_0=6,\;s_1=16,\;s_2=27,\dots$.  Prove that some term $s_n$ ends with the
four decimal digits \textbf{8715}.
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  A convenient way to group the steps
-------------------------------------
For $n\ge 0$ let
$$a_n\le b_n\le c_n,\qquad s_n=a_n+b_n+c_n.$$
Group the steps in blocks of three:
$$\text{block }n:\;\{s_{3n},s_{3n+1},s_{3n+2}\}\qquad(n\ge0).$$

Claim (a).
For every $n\ge0$ the nine *summands* appearing in block $n$
$$a_{3n},b_{3n},c_{3n},\;a_{3n+1},b_{3n+1},c_{3n+1},\;a_{3n+2},b_{3n+2},c_{3n+2}$$
are exactly the ten consecutive integers $10n+1,10n+2,\dots ,10n+10$ with
*one (and only one)* of the three middle numbers $10n+5,10n+6,10n+7$ missing.

Claim (b) (an immediate consequence of (a))
$$\begin{aligned}
s_{3n}   &=30n+6,\\[2pt]
s_{3n+1} &\in\{30n+15,\,30n+16,\,30n+17\},\\[2pt]
s_{3n+2} &=30n+27.\end{aligned}$$

Proof of Claim (a) by induction on $n$
--------------------------------------
Base case $n=0$.  Direct computation gives
$$(a_0,b_0,c_0)=(1,2,3),\;s_0=6;\quad(a_1,b_1,c_1)=(4,5,7),\;s_1=16;\quad
(a_2,b_2,c_2)=(8,9,10),\;s_2=27,$$
so the nine summands are $\{1,\dots ,10\}\setminus\{6\}$, as required.

Induction step $n\to n+1$.  Assume (a)$_n$ (hence also (b)$_n$) is true.

(i)   All integers $\le10n+10$ have already been crossed off.  Indeed they are
removed either as original integers in earlier blocks or as the unique missing
middle number of block $n$, which was crossed out earlier as a *sum*.

(ii)  Every sum produced so far ends in the digit $5,6$, or $7$.

    Proof of (ii).
    By the induction hypothesis we know (b)$_k$ for all $k\le n$.  That formula
    shows that $s_{3k}$ ends in $6$, $s_{3k+2}$ in $7$, and $s_{3k+1}$ in
    $5,6$, or $7$.  No other sums have been produced.

Because of (ii) no previously produced sum can coincide with a positive integer
ending in $1,2$, or $3$.  Hence, at the beginning of block $n+1$ the first
numbers that are *still present* and *have never been a sum* are exactly
$$10(n+1)+1,\;10(n+1)+2,\;10(n+1)+3,$$
so they are $a_{3(n+1)},b_{3(n+1)},c_{3(n+1)}$ and form the sum
$$s_{3(n+1)}=(10(n+1)+1)+(10(n+1)+2)+(10(n+1)+3)=30(n+1)+6.$$

Removing them leaves the four consecutive numbers
$$10(n+1)+4,\;10(n+1)+5,\;10(n+1)+6,\;10(n+1)+7,$$
exactly one of the last three having been the missing middle number of an
earlier block.  The next sum therefore uses the *other* three of these four
numbers, establishing the description of $s_{3(n+1)+1}$.  Finally the numbers
$10(n+1)+8,10(n+1)+9,10(n+1)+10$ are the three smallest remaining, so they form
$s_{3(n+1)+2}$.  This completes the induction step and thereby the proof of
Claim (a).

2.  A recursion for the choice $s_{3m+1}$
----------------------------------------
Define
$$f(m):=s_{3m+1}-(30m+16)\in\{-1,0,1\}\qquad(m\ge0).$$
Interpreting Claim (a) yields
$$
  f(m)=\begin{cases}
     0 &\text{if }10m+6 \text{ is the missing middle number},\\[2pt]
    -1 &\text{if }10m+7 \text{ is missing},\\[2pt]
     1 &\text{if }10m+5 \text{ is missing}.
  \end{cases}
$$

Lemma.  For every $m\ge0$
$$
  f(m)=\begin{cases}
          0                         & m\equiv0\pmod3,\\[4pt]
         -f\!\left(\dfrac{m-1}{3}\right) & m\equiv1\pmod3,\\[10pt]
         -1                        & m\equiv2\pmod3.\tag{1}
       \end{cases}
$$

Proof.
Compare block $m$ with block $\lfloor m/3\rfloor$.
* If $m\equiv0$, the exceptional number is $10m+6$, so $f(m)=0$.
* If $m\equiv2$, the exceptional number is $10m+7$, so $f(m)=-1$.
* If $m\equiv1$, write $m=3q+1$.  The numbers $10q+5,10q+6,10q+7$ are treated
in block $q$; hence $10m+5=30q+15$ is either already a sum or is still present
when block $m$ begins. A direct inspection of block $m$ shows that the opposite
choice is made for its new exceptional number, whence $f(3q+1)=-f(q)$. \blacksquare 

A useful corollary of (1) is the implication
$$m\equiv2\pmod3\;\Longrightarrow\;f(m)=-1.\tag{2}$$
Consequently, by Claim (b)
$$m\equiv2\pmod3\;\Longrightarrow\;s_{3m+1}=30m+15.\tag{3}$$

3.  Producing the digits 8715
-----------------------------
A number ends with $8715$ precisely when it is congruent to $8715\pmod{10000}$,
hence to $15\pmod{30}$.  By Claim (b) such a remainder occurs only among the
terms $s_{3m+1}$, so we need
$$30m+15\equiv8715\pmod{10000}.\tag{4}$$
Condition (4) is equivalent to
$$30m\equiv8700\pmod{10000}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;3m\equiv870\pmod{1000}.$$
Because $\gcd(3,1000)=1$, multiplication by $3^{-1}\equiv667\pmod{1000}$ gives
$$m\equiv290\pmod{1000}.\tag{5}$$
Note that $290\equiv2\pmod3$, so by (2)-(3) every integer of the form
$$m=290+1000k\qquad(k\ge0)$$
satisfies $s_{3m+1}=30m+15$.  Taking $k=0$ gives the concrete choice
$$m=290,\quad n=3m+1=871.$$
Substituting in (3) yields
$$s_{871}=30\cdot290+15=8715.$$
Thus the term $s_{871}$ indeed ends with the digits $8715$, completing the
proof.  \blacksquare

core_steps: Detect a repeating 3-step pattern by checking the first few eliminations.
Inductively prove that each block of three steps removes the ten integers 10n+1,…,10n+10 except one of 10n+5,6,7.
Consequently obtain explicit formulas for the sums: s_{3n}=30n+6, s_{3n+1}∈{30n+15,30n+16,30n+17}, s_{3n+2}=30n+27.
Use modular arithmetic to pick n=3m+1 with m≡2 (mod 3) and 30m+15 ≡ desired_suffix (mod 10 000).
Exhibit any m satisfying those congruences to produce a term ending with the required digits.

slot_suffix.description: The specific trailing base-10 digits we want a sum to end with; only the modulus in step 4 changes.

slot_witness.description: Concrete choice of m (hence n and the sum value) solving the congruences; many possibilities exist.
